description: This image presents the official CrossMark logo, a standardized identifier for scholarly publications. It is composed of a blue circular outline encapsulating a red bookmark-shaped graphic, with the text 'CrossMark' printed in black serif font on the right side of the graphic. This logo is not a material science experimental or computational result; it functions to indicate that readers can obtain the latest updates, corrections, and associated metadata of the linked 2017 review paper on Fe-Mn-Al-C low density steels through this mark. No material characterization data, performance curves, or structural schematics related to the paper's research content are included in this figure.

This figure consists of two subplots illustrating the effect of alloying elements on properties of ferritic iron and Fe-Al alloys for low-density steel development. Subplot (a) shows density (unit: g/cm³) of ferritic iron as a function of alloy content (0 to 16 wt%) for elements lighter than Fe, including C, Al, Si, Mn and Cr: all these elements reduce density, with reduction magnitude following the order C > Al > Si > Mn > Cr, where 16 wt% Al addition reduces density from ~7.87 g/cm³ to ~6.1 g/cm³. Subplot (b) presents Young's modulus (unit: GPa) of annealed Fe-Al steels as a function of Al content (0 to 16 wt%), with four independent literature datasets (Frommeyer, Kubo, Yamamoto-Taniguchi, Fukuroi-Shibuya) all showing a consistent decreasing trend: Young's modulus reduces by 2-2.5% per 1 wt% Al addition, dropping to ~140 GPa when Al content reaches 12 wt%. The core conclusion supported is that light alloying elements like Al effectively lower steel density for lightweight design, but simultaneously cause significant Young's modulus reduction, which is a critical trade-off for low-density steel application in automotive structural parts.

This plot presents the Young's modulus (in GPa) of Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density steels as a function of Mn content (in wt%, ranging from 5 wt% to 40 wt%), tested via the resonance method. Three processing states are compared: 1) as-cast state (blue curve, Young's modulus ranges 140-152 GPa), which shows the lowest modulus due to casting defects like shrinkage cavities and dendritic segregation; 2) as-cold rolled and annealed state (grey shaded band, modulus ranges 157-166 GPa), with slightly lower modulus than the hot rolled state attributed to cold deformation effect and texture evolution during annealing; 3) as-hot rolled state (red curve, modulus ranges 168-180 GPa), which has the highest modulus as hot rolling eliminates micro-cavities from the cast structure. The core conclusion is that Young's modulus of Fe-Mn-Al-C steels strongly depends on processing routes, and the modulus of cold rolled Fe-Mn-Al-C strips is approximately 20% lower than that of conventional high strength low alloy (HSLA) steels (~210 GPa).

This bar chart compares the specific stiffness normalized to that of 6xxx series aluminium alloy of four material systems: 6xxx series Aluminium, HSLA steel, ferritic Fe-8Al low density steel, and austenitic Fe-22Mn-8Al-0.8C low density steel. Three different specific stiffness indexes for different structural part applications are set as x-axis variables: E/ρ for tie structures where n=1, √E/ρ for beam structures where n=1/2, and ³√E/ρ for panel structures where n=1/3, with E referring to Young's modulus and ρ referring to density. The results show that HSLA steel has approximately 10% higher E/ρ value than aluminium, while both Fe-8Al and Fe-22Mn-8Al-0.8C low density steels exhibit lower specific stiffness than HSLA steel and aluminium across all three indexes. This figure supports the conclusion that low density Fe-Mn-Al-C steels are not suitable for structural parts with strict stiffness limitations, and they should be applied to stiffness-insensitive parts to achieve weight saving.

This figure presents the Fe-rich part of the binary Fe-Al phase diagram from Kubaschewski. The x-axis denotes Al weight percentage ranging from 0 to 40 wt%, and the y-axis denotes temperature ranging from 200 to 1600 ℃. Distinguished phase regions cover liquid phase L, γ austenite, disordered α (A2) ferrite, ordered FeAl (B2), ordered Fe₃Al (DO₃), ε phase, FeAl₂, plus two-phase regions including L+α, α+FeAl, α+Fe₃Al, FeAl+ε, FeAl+FeAl₂. Key transition temperatures are marked: 1536 ℃ (pure Fe melting point), 1392 ℃ and 911 ℃ (γ phase transition points), 1310 ℃, 1215 ℃, 1092 ℃, 552 ℃ for peritectic, eutectoid and order-disorder transitions. A dashed magnetic transformation line and K-state regions (K1, K2) are also included. This diagram guides phase evolution analysis for Fe-Al based low density steels, verifying that higher Al content and lower temperature promote A2→B2→DO₃ ordered phase formation.

This figure displays three pseudo-binary phase diagrams illustrating the effects of Mn and Al addition on phase constituents and phase fields of the Fe-C system, with x-axis representing C content in wt% (0 to 4) and y-axis representing temperature in ℃ (200 to 1600). The first diagram (a) is the standard Fe-C phase diagram, containing phase regions of L, L+δ, L+γ, γ, α, γ+M₃C, α+M₃C, marked with eutectoid point S and eutectic point E. The second diagram (b) is the Fe-15Mn-C phase diagram: the single γ phase field extends to lower temperatures, the temperature of point S decreases, and extra carbide phases M₇C₃, M₂₃C₆ plus three-phase regions like α+γ+M₃C appear at low temperatures. The third diagram (c) is the Fe-7Al-C phase diagram: the δ+γ phase region is enlarged, the single γ phase region shifts to higher C content, the original γ+M₃C region is replaced by γ+κ and γ+κ+M₃C regions, and the dashed line indicates the A2 to B2 order-disorder transition temperature (below 500℃, decreasing with increasing C content). This figure demonstrates that Mn and Al significantly alter phase transition temperatures, phase field ranges and precipitate types of Fe-C alloys, providing thermodynamic basis for composition design and heat treatment of Fe-Mn-Al-C low density steels.

This group of three subfigures presents unit cell crystal structure models for phases in FCC-based Fe-Mn-Al-C low density steels. Subfigure (a) illustrates the disordered γ austenite unit cell, where Fe/Mn and Al atoms randomly occupy the cubic corner and face-centered positions of the FCC lattice. Subfigure (b) shows the ordered γ phase with L1₂ structure, in which Al atoms locate at all unit cell corners while Fe and Mn atoms occupy face-centered positions. Subfigure (c) demonstrates the ideal stoichiometric κ-carbide (perovskite E2₁ structure, nominal composition (Fe,Mn)₃AlC), with a C atom occupying the octahedral interstitial site at the body center of the unit cell. The figure systematically compares the structural evolution from disordered austenite to ordered κ-carbide via atomic site ordering, supporting the phase transformation and ordering mechanism conclusions for Fe-Mn-Al-C steel systems.

This set of figures presents 900 °C isothermal phase sections of Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density steels, with Mn content fixed at 10 wt%, 20 wt%, and 30 wt% for the three subplots respectively. The x-axis represents Al content (0-14 wt%), and the y-axis represents C content (0-4 wt%). Phase boundaries from three sources are plotted: solid lines for FactSage 6.4 calculation results, dotted lines for CALPHAD approach calculation results, and red lines for experimentally determined phase boundaries. Experimental alloy composition points are marked with different symbols: open circles for the γ phase field, open triangles for the γ+κ phase field, open squares for the γ+M₃C phase field, solid squares for the α+κ phase field, and solid diamonds for the B2+κ related phase field. Multiple phase regions including γ, γ+M₃C, γ+M₃C+κ, γ+κ, γ+κ+α, γ+α, α+κ, and B2+κ are identified. The figure compares deviations between calculated and experimental phase boundaries, validates thermodynamic database accuracy, and supports composition design of Fe-Mn-Al-C steels.

This group of plots presents the FactSage 6.4 calculated equilibrium phase proportions as a function of temperature (ranging from 400℃ to 850℃) for two Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density steels: Fe-13.5Mn-6.3Al-0.78C and Fe-18Mn-7Al-0.85C. Each plot contains curves for γ austenite (fraction increases with rising temperature, reaching nearly 100% above ~750℃), α ferrite (fraction decreases with rising temperature, dropping to nearly 0 above ~750℃), and carbide phases including M₂₃C₆, M₇C₃ and κ-carbide, which present in small fractions (peak at ~10%) at low to medium temperatures and dissolve at elevated temperatures. Comparison shows the Fe-18Mn-7Al-0.85C alloy with higher Mn, Al and C contents has higher peak κ-carbide fraction and lower stable temperature range for M₂₃C₆. These results demonstrate that temperature and alloy composition significantly affect equilibrium phase constitution of Fe-Mn-Al-C steels, providing thermodynamic reference for heat treatment design of such steels.

This group of figures displays three polythermal phase diagram sections of Fe-15wt%Mn-xAl-yC low-density steels calculated via FactSage software, with fixed Mn content of 15 wt% and varied Al contents: 4 wt% (first subplot), 7 wt% (second subplot), and 10 wt% (third subplot). All plots take carbon content (0 to 2.5 wt%) as the horizontal axis and temperature (200 to 1600 °C) as the vertical axis. Each section labels stable phase regions containing phases including liquid L, δ-ferrite, γ-austenite, α-ferrite, B2 ordered intermetallic, β phase, κ-carbide, and carbides M₃C, M₇C₃, M₂₃C₆. Dash-dotted lines represent the formation temperature of the j phase, while dashed lines indicate the disorder-order transition temperature. As Al content increases, the stable phase region of κ-carbide expands, the stability range of low-temperature ordered phases (e.g., B2) increases, and the stability ranges of different carbides change. These diagrams provide thermodynamic guidance for composition design and heat treatment optimization of Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density steels.

This figure displays two calculated polythermal phase diagram sections for Fe-Mn-Al-0.85C low-density steel system using FactSage. The first section keeps Mn content at 20 wt% and C at 0.85 wt%, with x-axis representing Al content from 0 to 12 wt% and y-axis for temperature from 200 to 1600 °C, marking phase regions including liquid L, austenite γ, δ/α ferrite, carbides M3C, M7C3, M23C6, ordered B2 phase, κ-carbide and β phase; dash-dotted lines indicate β phase formation temperature, while dashed lines represent disorder-to-order transition temperature. The second section fixes Al at 7 wt% and C at 0.85 wt%, with x-axis as Mn content from 0 to 30 wt%, showing phase stability ranges under different Mn concentrations. These diagrams offer clear thermodynamic guidance for composition design and heat treatment formulation of Fe-Mn-Al-C low density steels.

This figure displays process variants for producing hot-rolled (HR) and cold-rolled (CR) austenitic Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density steel strips, with 5 numbered process routes for different product requirements. The initial step is homogenization at 1100–1200°C, followed by hot rolling with finish rolling temperature (FRT) controlled at 900°C. For hot-rolled products: Route 1 adopts fast cooling to 500–750°C then slow cooling/isothermal holding; Route 2 uses fast cooling to room temperature followed by isothermal annealing, with cooling rates designed to avoid detrimental intergranular κ-carbide precipitation. For cold-rolled products: after cold rolling, Route 3 is 900–1100°C solution treatment followed by direct quenching; Routes 4 and 5 add 500–700°C aging for 5–20 h to maximize κ'-carbide precipitation hardening, while 600–900°C short-time recrystallization annealing is applied for non-age-hardenable grades to restore ductility. This figure summarizes optimized processing pathways to tune the microstructure and performance of the studied steel system.

This figure presents the Time-Temperature-Transformation (TTT) diagram for γ solid solution decomposition during isothermal aging of Fe-28Mn-8.5Al-1C-1.25Si low-density steel. The x-axis is isothermal time in logarithmic scale ranging from 0.01 h to 1000 h, and the y-axis is aging temperature from 300 °C to 1000 °C. Five distinct phase regions are marked: Region 1 is single γ phase corresponding to spinodal decomposition; Region 2 is γ + κ' phase region, where homogeneous κ' carbide precipitates from the matrix; Region 3 is γ + κ' + κ* phase region, corresponding to grain boundary κ* carbide formation; Region 4 is γ + κ' + κ* + α(B2/DO₃) phase region, representing discontinuous grain boundary reaction products of B2/DO₃ structured α phase; Region 5 is γ + κ + B2/DO₃ phase region, where equilibrium phases form. The right side indicates ordered phase stability ranges: B2 phase at temperatures above ~750 °C, mixed B2 + DO₃ phase at intermediate temperatures, and DO₃ phase below ~650 °C. This diagram clarifies phase evolution paths under different isothermal aging conditions, providing guidance for heat treatment design to control microstructures of the alloy.

This group of secondary electron SEM images present the microstructure evolution of Fe-18Mn-7Al-0.85C austenitic steel after isothermal holding at 700℃ for different durations, to demonstrate the growth of κ-carbides and the formation of (κ + γ + α) lamellar colonies at austenite grain boundaries via cellular transformation. Subfigure (a), magnified ×20,000 with 1 μm scale bar, corresponds to 1 h holding, showing initial fine κ-carbide precipitation along grain boundaries. Subfigure (b), magnified ×10,000 with 1 μm scale bar, corresponds to 25 h holding, displaying distinguishable alternating lamellae of α-ferrite, γ-austenite and κ-carbide at grain boundaries. Subfigure (c), magnified ×2,000 with 10 μm scale bar, corresponds to 100 h holding, revealing well-grown large lamellar colonies expanding into austenite matrix. The figure validates that cellular transformation γ→γ+κ+α proceeds with grain boundary migration, and the volume and size of lamellar colonies increase with longer isothermal holding time.

This figure presents the microstructures of Fe-13.5Mn-6.3Al-0.78C low-density steel after solution treatment at 1000 °C followed by aging at 600 °C for 100 h. Subfigure (a) is an optical microscopy image with a 100 μm scale bar, showing large κ-pearlite colonies formed along austenite grain boundaries. Subfigures (b) and (c) are bright-field transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images with 0.2 μm scale bars, revealing the internal lamellar structure of κ-pearlites, where three phases are identified: α ferrite, κ-carbide (marked as K), and M₂₃C₆ carbide (marked as C). The figure validates that austenite undergoes eutectoid decomposition into α + κ + M₂₃C₆ during aging below 650 °C in this quaternary Fe-Mn-Al-C alloy, supporting the established phase transformation mechanism for this class of low-density steels.

This figure is a phase composition map for Fe-Mn-Al-C low density steels, with x-axis representing Al content (0 to 12 wt%) and y-axis representing C content (0 to 1.6 wt%). Three solid lines show the predicted κ-carbide formation boundaries for 15Mn, 20Mn, and 30Mn alloy systems, calculated from the formula reported by Huang et al. Two dashed lines denote the phase boundary between γ+α and γ+κ+α regions for 15Mn and 30Mn alloys, respectively. Experimental data points are marked as: open circles for alloy compositions located in single γ phase field, solid squares for compositions in γ+κ dual phase field, solid diamonds for compositions in γ+κ+α triple phase field. It reveals that higher Mn content shifts the κ-carbide formation boundary to higher Al content, and the C content required to stabilize austenite increases with rising Al content. This plot supports the composition design of Fe-Mn-Al-C steels to obtain target phase assemblages and corresponding properties.

This set of phase diagrams illustrates the effects of Al and Mn contents on κ-carbide solvus temperatures in Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density steels. The first subplot targets Fe-30Mn-xAl-1C alloys, with x-axis representing Al content (5-10 wt%) and y-axis representing temperature (500-900 °C), marking three phase regions: single γ (austenite), γ+κ* (γ + intergranular κ-carbide), and γ+κ*+κ' (γ + intergranular + intragranular κ-carbide). The solid and dashed lines are solvus boundaries, which rise significantly as Al content increases. The second subplot targets Fe-xMn-7Al-1C alloys, with x-axis representing Mn content (23-29 wt%), showing the same three phase regions, while solvus boundaries decrease slightly as Mn content increases. Experimental data points (cross, open circle, filled circle) validate the plotted boundaries. This figure confirms that Al addition substantially elevates both intergranular and intragranular κ-carbide solvus temperatures, while Mn addition causes mild reduction, providing guidance for composition and heat treatment design of Fe-Mn-Al-C steels.

This figure targets Fe–15Mn–10Al-0.86C–5Ni low-density steel processed by 70% thickness reduction cold rolling followed by 900 °C annealing for 2 min. It includes a microstructure micrograph with a 10 μm scale bar and a corresponding formation mechanism schematic of B2 precipitates. In the micrograph, Area (1) refers to B2 stringer bands transformed from solidification-derived δ ferrite; Area (2) corresponds to type 2 B2 particles (200–1000 nm) precipitated at γ austenite grain boundaries and edges; Area (3) corresponds to type 3 B2 particles (50–300 nm) precipitated at shear bands inside γ grains. The schematic illustrates that recrystallized deformed γ grains induce larger B2 precipitation at grain boundaries, while non-recrystallized deformed γ grains with shear bands induce finer B2 precipitation at shear bands during annealing. It confirms deformation substructures act as preferential B2 nucleation sites to regulate precipitate size, supporting B2 nanoprecipitation as an effective strengthening strategy for this steel system.

This figure contains two micrographs demonstrating phase evolution in Fe-Mn-Al-C low density steels during hot processing. Subfigure (a) is an optical micrograph with a 10 μm scale bar (inset has a 25 μm scale bar), showing α-ferrite formed at hot rolling temperatures in the γ phase band of Fe-5Mn-6Al-0.4C steel, as well as α+κ phases formed at coiling temperatures; the inset also marks δ band and γ phase band. Subfigure (b) is a SEM micrograph taken at 1.46 kX magnification, 20.00 kV EHT, with a 10 μm scale bar, revealing κ-carbide precipitated along δ/γ boundaries to form a shell structure surrounding γ phase in Fe-2Mn-8Al-0.2C steel during hot rolling. This figure supports the conclusion that γ→α phase transformation occurs during hot rolling, and κ-carbide can precipitate at hot working temperatures in low Mn Fe-Mn-Al-C steels, while increased Mn content avoids the formation of such coarse κ-carbides.

This set of micrographs presents the microstructures of an air-cooled hot-rolled Fe–9Mn–6Al–0.15C low-density steel specimen. Subfigure (a) is an optical micrograph with a 100 μm scale bar, showing banded structure elongated along the rolling direction, consisting of partially recrystallized white δ-ferrite, brown γ-austenite, and gray lath-like mixed phases of α'+α+γ. Subfigure (b) is a scanning electron microscopy (SEM) image with a 10 μm scale bar, revealing the fine details inside the lath-like phase, including γ-austenite, lath-shaped α'-martensite, and small globular α-ferrite particles. No κ-carbide precipitates are observed in the samples, verifying that with Mn content ≥6 wt%, the austenite stability is improved, suppressing the γ→α+κ phase transformation during air cooling after hot rolling, and part of austenite transforms to martensite while retained austenite is stabilized at room temperature.

This group of two plots illustrates mechanical properties of ferritic Fe-Al low-density steels as a function of Al content (0 to 25 wt%), with corresponding crystal structure phase regions (A2, K1, A2+DO₃, DO₃, B2) marked. Subplot (a) displays tensile properties: yield strength (YS, blue diamonds), ultimate tensile strength (UTS, open circles), total elongation (TE, red triangles), and uniform elongation (UE, black triangles). YS and UTS first increase with rising Al content, peak at ~13 wt% Al, then drop sharply between 13-15 wt% Al and remain stable at higher Al, while both TE and UE decrease gradually with Al, with a ductile-to-brittle transition occurring at 8-10 wt% Al. Subplot (b) presents work hardening rate measured between 1% and 1.5% plastic strain: it stays low for Al <7 wt%, rises to a sharp peak at ~16 wt% Al corresponding to ordered phase formation, then decreases to moderate values above 20 wt% Al. This figure verifies that Al solid solution strengthening and ordered structural transitions synergistically regulate the mechanical performance of Fe-Al steels.

This figure presents two groups of engineering stress-strain curves of austenitic and austenite-based duplex Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density steels. The first subplot demonstrates the effect of Al and C contents: all tested samples are treated by 1000℃/1h solutionizing followed by water quenching, including 20Mn-10Al-1.5C, 20Mn-11Al-1.0C, 20Mn-10Al-1.0C, and 22Mn-xAl-0.6C (x=0,3,6) series. It shows that increasing Al content reduces yield strength, ultimate tensile strength and strain hardening rate, while the Al-free 22Mn-0Al-0.6C steel exhibits the highest strength among the 22Mn series. The second subplot shows the effect of κ'-carbide and B2 intermetallic phases: tested alloys include 28Mn-12Al-1.0C under water quenching, 550℃ aging for 5/46 min, 18Mn-10Al-0.9C-5Ni under 900℃ holding for 2/15 min, and 28Mn-10Al-1.0C water quenched. Longer aging time increases yield strength but reduces total elongation, the B2-containing Ni-alloyed steel shows ultrahigh strength, and 28Mn-10Al-1.0C reaches ~100% elongation. The figure confirms that composition and heat treatment (precipitation regulation) significantly tune the strength-ductility trade-off of this steel system, and high Al/C steels show unique low or even negative work hardening rate while retaining good ductility.

This figure presents true stress and strain hardening rate versus true strain curves from room-temperature tensile tests of various Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density steels for deformation mechanism investigation. The tested alloys include TWIP steel Fe-22Mn-0.6C, low-Al Fe-30Mn-2Al-1.2C, Fe-28Mn-10Al-1C corresponding to microband induced plasticity (MBIP), Fe-30Mn-8Al-1.2C corresponding to dynamic slip band refinement (DSBR), Fe-27Mn-12Al-0.9C corresponding to shear band induced plasticity (SIP), and Fe-15Mn-9Al-0.7C. Key trends: the TWIP steel shows a typical 'hump' in strain hardening rate related to deformation twinning-induced microstructure refinement; low-Al steel exhibits multi-stage strain hardening; high-Al MBIP, DSBR, SIP steels also show similar hardening rate humps but rely on different deformation mechanisms associated with short-range ordering (SRO) or κ-carbides, while Fe-15Mn-9Al-0.7C shows monotonic decay of strain hardening rate. This figure supports that strain hardening behaviors of Fe-Mn-Al-C austenitic steels vary with Al content and corresponding deformation mechanisms, and existing mechanisms cannot fully explain all cases such as negative strain hardening rate in peak-aged steels.

This group of 3D plots presents the room-temperature composition-dependent stacking fault energy (SFE, unit: mJ/m²) maps and corresponding deformation mechanism classification for Fe-Mn-Al-C low density steels. Subplot (a) illustrates SFE as a function of C (0-1.2 wt%) and Mn (10-50 wt%) contents at fixed Al levels (3, 5, 8, 11 wt%). Subplot (b) shows SFE as a function of Al (0-12 wt%) and Mn (10-50 wt%) contents at fixed C levels (0, 0.3, 0.6, 0.9, 1.2, 1.5 wt%). Subplot (c) maps SFE to deformation regimes, including TRIP (SFE < 20 mJ/m²), TWIP (20 < SFE < 50 mJ/m²), MBIP (SFE > 50 mJ/m²), and kappa-carbide forming regions. Key trends: Al and C additions linearly increase SFE (10 mJ/m² per wt% Al, 40 mJ/m² per wt% C), while Mn decreases SFE at 0-20 wt% and increases SFE at higher contents. The figure supports that SFE calculation from composition can predict deformation mechanisms, guiding composition optimization of Fe-Mn-Al-C steels for target mechanical performance.

This figure plots total elongation (TE, %) as a function of ultimate tensile strength (UTS, MPa) for Fe-Mn-Al-C low density steels, tested with standard ASTM E-8 geometry specimens made from solution-treated and water-quenched strips. The plot divides performance regions corresponding to different microstructures: δ single phase zone (including IF, mild, BH, CMn, HSLA steels, UTS 200-600 MPa, TE 10-40%), δ based duplex zone, TRIP/DP/CP steel zone (UTS 400-900 MPa, TE 10-40%), γ based duplex zone (UTS 600-1200 MPa, TE 30-70%), γ single phase zone (including Mn-TWIP steels, UTS 600-1000 MPa, maximum TE up to ~100%), and γ+(δ)+ coarse κ carbide martensitic steel zone (UTS 900-1800 MPa, TE <15%). Diagonal contour lines represent strength-ductility product (in GPa%), with γ single phase TWIP steels achieving the highest value up to 70 GPa%. The overall trend shows that strength-ductility matching improves significantly as the microstructure shifts from δ ferrite dominant to γ austenite dominant, providing a performance reference for steel grade selection for different applications.

This figure presents engineering stress-strain curves of ferritic and ferrite-based duplex low-density Fe-Mn-Al-C steels after different heat treatments. The tested alloys include two ferritic steels (Fe-6.8Al, Fe-8.1Al, annealed at 850°C for 5min, from reference [41]), one ferrite-based duplex steel Fe-3.3Mn-6.6Al-0.2C treated by 800°C/5min annealing and 800°C/5min + 400°C/10min austempering (from reference [44]), and another duplex steel Fe-8.5Mn-5.6Al-0.3C treated at 800°C for 1min, 800°C for 30min, and 900°C for 30min (from reference [51]). The y-axis is engineering stress in MPa, and x-axis is engineering strain in %. Ferritic steels show ultimate tensile strength (UTS) of 450-520 MPa with total elongation (TE) of 15-30%. The duplex steels exhibit wider performance ranges: Fe-8.5Mn-5.6Al-0.3C annealed at 900°C for 30min delivers the best strength-ductility balance with UTS ~720 MPa and TE ~75%, and austempered Fe-3.3Mn-6.6Al-0.2C shows higher elongation than directly annealed counterparts. This figure supports that composition and heat treatment adjustment can tune mechanical properties of low-density steels in a wide range, and ferrite-based duplex steels achieve excellent mechanical performance via TRIP/TWIP effects.

This figure presents tensile properties of two Fe-Mn-Al-C low density steel series as a function of Al content (0 to 12 wt%). The first subplot corresponds to Fe-30Mn-1.2C-xAl alloys, while the second corresponds to Fe-30Mn-0.4C-xAl alloys, all solutionized at 1100 °C for 2 h. Solid curves represent water-quenched samples, while dotted curves represent samples that are water-quenched then aged at 550 °C for 24 h in air. Plotted properties include yield strength (YS, MPa), ultimate tensile strength (UTS, MPa, left y-axis), total elongation (TE, %), and δ-ferrite fraction (%, right y-axis). Key trends: For the 1.2C series, aging induces significant hardening only when Al content exceeds 8 wt%, which is attributed to κ'-carbide precipitation. For the 0.4C series, YS and UTS decrease while TE increases as Al rises from 0 to 2 wt% (attributed to fully austenitic microstructure formation and increased stacking fault energy), followed by increasing strength and decreasing TE with further Al addition, as δ-ferrite fraction increases linearly with Al. Aging has negligible effect on tensile properties of the 0.4C series. This figure demonstrates the combined effects of C content, Al addition, and aging treatment on the tensile performance of Fe-Mn-Al-C steels, supporting composition and process optimization for desirable strength-ductility balance.

This figure presents tensile properties of two Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density steel series as a function of carbon (C) content ranging from 0 to 2 wt%. The first subplot corresponds to Fe-30Mn-9Al-xC alloy, while the second corresponds to Fe-20Mn-10Al-xC alloy. Curves include yield strength (YS, MPa, dark blue diamonds), ultimate tensile strength (UTS, MPa, magenta squares) mapped to the left Y-axis, and total elongation (TE, %, black triangles) plus δ-ferrite volume fraction (δ, %, red crosses) mapped to the right Y-axis. Key observed trends: δ-ferrite content decreases continuously with rising C and disappears at ~0.7 wt% C for Fe-30Mn-9Al-xC and ~1.0 wt% C for Fe-20Mn-10Al-xC, when the matrix transforms to single-phase austenite. TE rises to a maximum at the duplex-to-single-phase transition point then decreases with further C addition. YS and UTS show limited change in the duplex region, and increase monotonically with C in single-phase austenitic steels. The results demonstrate that C content tunes phase composition and κ-carbide precipitation to optimize the strength-ductility tradeoff in this steel family.

This figure includes two plots exploring mechanical properties of austenite-based Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density steels. The first plot presents the effect of Al content (3 to 10 wt%) on properties of Fe-30Mn-xAl-(0.85, 0.95)C steels, which were water quenched from 1150 °C and aged at 550 °C for 16 h. It plots ultimate tensile strength (UTS, triangular markers), yield strength (YS, open triangular markers), U-notch Charpy impact energy (E_CUN, diamond markers), total elongation (TE, solid circular markers) and δ-ferrite fraction (cross markers) for both 0.95 wt% C (solid lines) and 0.85 wt% C (dashed lines) specimens. The second plot shows the effect of C content (0.4 to 1.2 wt%) on Fe-30Mn-9Al-xC steel properties, with dashed lines for 1050 °C water quenched samples and solid lines for samples aged at 550 °C for 16 h. Key trends: UTS and YS continuously increase when Al content exceeds 7 wt% and C content rises; E_CUN and TE reach their peaks at ~6 wt% Al and ~0.7 wt% C respectively, then decrease at higher Al/C content, while δ-ferrite is only detected at Al >8 wt% and reduces with increasing C. This figure supports that aged Fe-Mn-Al-C alloys with Al >7 wt% and C >0.7 wt% achieve significant strengthening from dispersed nano-sized κ-carbide precipitates, accompanied by reduced ductility and impact toughness at higher alloying levels.

This is a semilogarithmic plot comparing the precipitation strengthening effects of κ-carbides in Fe-Mn-Al-C low density steels and common microalloying carbides (NbC, VC, TiC) applied in conventional microalloyed steels. The x-axis represents precipitation fraction with a logarithmic scale spanning from 0.0001 to 1, while the y-axis represents precipitation strengthening value in MPa ranging from 0 to 500. NbC provides 70-180 MPa strengthening at a precipitation fraction of 0.0003-0.001, VC provides 0-130 MPa at a fraction of ~0.001-0.002, TiC provides 120-450 MPa at a fraction of ~0.001-0.003, and κ-carbide provides 100-340 MPa at a fraction of 0.05-0.35. The figure verifies that κ-carbide can deliver considerable precipitation strengthening effect for Fe-Mn-Al-C steels, acting as a critical strengthening phase for this alloy system despite requiring a higher precipitation volume fraction than conventional microalloying carbides.

This figure presents aging property curves of austenitic Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density steels, consisting of three subplots: (a) Brinell hardness of Fe-30Mn-8Al-1C-0.35Si steel as a function of aging time (from water quenching (WQ) to 1000 h) at 450℃, 500℃, 550℃, 600℃, showing that higher aging temperature accelerates age-hardening kinetics, leading to earlier peak hardness followed by overaging softening. (b) Brinell hardness curves of multiple Fe-Mn-Al-C alloys with varied Al and C contents aged at 550℃, demonstrating that higher Al and C contents induce more significant age-hardening effect and higher peak hardness. (c) Tensile properties (ultimate tensile strength (UTS), yield strength (YS), total elongation (TE)) of several alloys as a function of aging time, where strength first increases to a peak then decreases, while elongation decreases continuously with prolonged aging, and higher C content contributes to higher peak strength. The core conclusion is that aging temperature and Al/C contents strongly regulate the age-hardening behavior and mechanical properties of this steel system, and a wide range of strength-ductility combinations can be achieved via processing control for automotive applications.

This figure presents two sets of engineering stress-strain curves for annealed Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density steels. The first subplot shows curves for Fe–22.5Mn–1.2Al–0.3C steel subjected to 30% or 50% cold rolling (CR) followed by different annealing treatments (630℃/10min, 700℃/15min for CR30%; 550℃/30min, 700℃/10min for CR50%). The second subplot displays curves for Fe-22Mn-7Al-0.8C steel after 50% cold rolling and recrystallization annealing at 800-975℃ for 1min to 25min. Obvious trends are observed: higher cold rolling reduction, lower annealing temperature, or shorter annealing duration leads to higher tensile strength but lower ductility (elongation). As annealing temperature or time increases, the recrystallized austenite fraction rises, improving ductility while reducing strength. The figure supports the conclusion that cold rolling combined with optimized post-annealing processes can effectively tune the strength-ductility balance of Fe-Mn-Al-C steels via grain refinement and microstructure control.

This figure plots Charpy V-notch (CVN) impact energy (unit: J) as a function of test temperature (range: -200°C to 100°C, unit: °C) for different Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density steels, with background shaded regions representing the impact energy ranges of austenitic, duplex, ferritic, and martensitic stainless steels for comparison. There are 5 tested curves: Curve 1 corresponds to Fe-28Mn-5Al-1C steel solutionized at 1050°C for 75 min (grain size 41 μm); Curve 2 is the same alloy solutionized at 1050°C for 8 h (grain size 135 μm); Curve 3 is Fe-30Mn-10Al-1C-1Si steel solutionized at 1050°C for 1 h (grain size 120 μm). These three solution-treated samples show high room-temperature impact energy (~200 J, comparable to austenitic stainless steels) and ductile-to-brittle transition temperature below -100°C. Curve 4 is the Curve 2 alloy after aging at 550°C for 16 h, with significantly reduced impact energy (~55 J at room temperature, decreasing continuously with falling temperature); Curve 5 is the Curve 3 alloy after the same aging treatment, with near-zero impact energy across all tested temperatures. The core conclusion is that as-solutionized austenitic Fe-Mn-Al-C steels possess excellent impact toughness matching austenitic stainless steels, while aging-induced κ-carbide precipitation leads to dramatic toughness deterioration, especially for Si-containing high-Al alloys which become nearly fully brittle after peak aging.

This is a mechanical property plot presenting specific energy absorption (unit: J/mm³) as a function of Al content (wt%, ranging from 6 to 12 wt%) for Fe-Mn-Al-C low density steels and conventional deep-drawing steels at crash-relevant high strain rates. The hollow square symbols correspond to three conventional deep-drawing steels (IF steel, HC300LA, DC04) tested at deformation rate of 10³/s, with their specific energy absorption values between 0.16 and 0.25 J/mm³. The diamond symbols represent Fe-Mn-Al-C steels tested at deformation rate of 10⁴/s: when Al content exceeds 7 wt%, their specific energy absorption reaches around 0.43-0.56 J/mm³, ~0.5 J/mm³ on average, which is 50% higher than conventional deep-drawing steels, comparable to Mn TWIP steels. The figure supports the core conclusion that Fe-Mn-Al-C steels exhibit excellent high-strain-rate energy absorption performance suitable for automotive crash safety structural applications, attributed to severe adiabatic shear band formation under high strain rate loading.

This figure presents the cyclic fatigue response of Fe-Mn-Al-C low density steels, with x-axis as number of cycles (logarithmic scale, 10^0 to 10^5) and y-axis as stress amplitude (MPa, 200 to 1100). Dotted curves represent Fe-30Mn-9Al-1C alloy subjected to three aging treatments (550℃/24h, 710℃/2h, 550℃/2h) tested under 0.8% strain amplitude, all exhibiting cyclic softening behavior: the 550℃/24h aged sample has the highest initial stress amplitude (~1030 MPa), which decreases to ~680 MPa at 10^4 cycles, higher than the two 2h aged counterparts. Solid curves correspond to solution-treated Fe-30Mn-10Al-0.4C alloy tested under total strain amplitudes ranging from 0.2% to 2.0%: for strain amplitudes below 0.6% (0.2%, 0.4%), direct cyclic softening is observed; for strain amplitudes above 0.6% (1.0%, 1.5%, 2.0%), cyclic strengthening occurs first before softening, with higher peak stress at higher strain amplitude. The core conclusion is that aging process and applied strain amplitude significantly dominate the fatigue cyclic behavior of Fe-Mn-Al-C steels, and cyclic softening is mainly attributed to shearing of κ-carbides and formation of persistent slip bands.
